title: Internal Analysis: RBV/VRIO, Value Chain & IFE
simple_html:
Internal analysis looks inward to find a firm's strengths (what it does well) and weaknesses (where it falls short). The goal is to discover what makes the company special — and hard to copy.

The big idea

Two firms in the same industry can perform very differently. Why? Because they own different resources and capabilities. Internal analysis finds the few resources that actually create lasting advantage.

Three core tools

  • RBV (Resource-Based View) says competitive advantage comes from valuable resources that rivals lack. It is the theory behind looking inward.
  • VRIO is the test: is a resource Valuable, Rare, costly to Imitate, and is the firm Organized to exploit it? Only resources that pass all four give sustained advantage.
  • Value Chain breaks the firm into activities (from inbound logistics to after-sales service) to see where it adds value and cuts cost.
  • IFE Matrix (Internal Factor Evaluation) scores the firm's strengths and weaknesses into one number from 1 to 4.

Key takeaways

  • Advantage often comes from inside, not just industry choice.
  • VRIO: only valuable + rare + hard-to-imitate + organized resources win long-term.
  • The value chain shows where you create value and where to improve.
  • An IFE score above 2.5 means the firm is internally strong.
intermediate_html:
Internal analysis (the "internal audit") identifies a firm's strengths and weaknesses across all functions. Where external analysis (Lesson 2) explains industry attractiveness, internal analysis explains why some firms outperform rivals in the same industry. Three tools dominate.

1. Resource-Based View (RBV)

RBV (Barney, 1991) argues that sustained competitive advantage stems from a firm's bundle of resources (tangible and intangible assets) and capabilities (the skills to deploy them), provided these are heterogeneous (firms differ) and immobile (hard to transfer). The strategic question shifts from "What industry should we be in?" to "What can we do better than anyone, and how do we leverage it?"

2. VRIO — the diagnostic test

VRIO evaluates each resource on four questions:

QuestionIf NOIf YES (all four)
Valuable?Competitive disadvantageSustained competitive advantage
Rare?Competitive parity
Costly to Imitate?Temporary advantage
Organized to capture value?Advantage unrealized

A resource must clear every gate. Brand reputation, patents, tacit know-how, and culture often pass; commodity inputs do not.

3. Porter's Value Chain

Porter (1985) splits the firm into primary activities (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing and sales, service) and support activities (firm infrastructure, HR management, technology development, procurement). Analyzing each reveals where value is created and where margin leaks. Linkages between activities (e.g., better design reducing service costs) are frequent sources of advantage that rivals overlook.

4. IFE Matrix

The Internal Factor Evaluation matrix mirrors the EFE: list 15-20 key internal factors (strengths then weaknesses), assign weights (sum to 1.0), rate each (where for IFE 4 = major strength, 3 = minor strength, 2 = minor weakness, 1 = major weakness), then sum weighted scores. The total ranges 1.0-4.0, averaging 2.5; above 2.5 signals an internally strong organization.

Worked example (VRIO)

A specialty pharmacy's proprietary cold-chain logistics network: Valuable? Yes (enables temperature-sensitive drug delivery). Rare? Yes (few rivals have it). Costly to imitate? Yes (years of investment and regulatory approvals). Organized? Yes (integrated with its order system). Verdict: sustained competitive advantage — this is a resource to build strategy around.

How to apply it

  • Use the value chain to generate candidate strengths/weaknesses, then run RBV/VRIO to find which are true advantages.
  • Feed the surviving factors into the IFE matrix to prioritize.
  • Pair IFE (internal) with EFE (external) to position the firm on the IE matrix (Lesson 6).
  • Remember: a strength that is valuable but not rare only gives parity, not advantage.
full_html:
Internal analysis explains intra-industry performance differences: why, facing the same five forces, one firm earns superior returns and another struggles. The answer lies inside the firm — in its resources, capabilities, and the activities through which it competes. This lesson develops the resource-based view and its VRIO diagnostic, Porter's value chain, and the IFE matrix that quantifies the resulting strengths and weaknesses, completing the internal half of SWOT.

The resource-based view

The RBV's intellectual lineage runs from Penrose (1959), who conceived the firm as a bundle of productive resources, through Wernerfelt (1984), who reframed strategy in terms of resources rather than products, to Barney (1991), who formalized the conditions for sustained advantage. Barney's VRIN criteria — Valuable, Rare, Imperfectly imitable, and Non-substitutable — specify when resource heterogeneity yields rents. He later operationalized these as the VRIO framework (Barney, 1995), replacing non-substitutability with Organization: the firm must be structured to actually exploit the resource. Isolating mechanisms that protect imitability include causal ambiguity (rivals cannot tell what drives success), path dependence (advantages accumulated over a unique history), and social complexity (culture, relationships) (Dierickx & Cool, 1989).

VRIO as a decision tree

VRIO is best read sequentially. If a resource is not valuable, it is a weakness (competitive disadvantage). If valuable but not rare, it yields only competitive parity. If valuable and rare but cheap to imitate, it grants a temporary advantage that erodes. If valuable, rare, and costly to imitate but the firm is not organized to capture it, the advantage goes unrealized (Barney terms this the "adjustment" question). Only when all four hold does the resource confer sustained competitive advantage. A frequent error is labeling any strength a source of advantage; rigorous VRIO usually disqualifies most.

Dynamic capabilities

RBV has been criticized as static — useful for explaining existing advantage but weak on how firms renew it in changing environments. Teece, Pisano, and Shuen (1997) answered with dynamic capabilities: the firm's ability to sense opportunities, seize them, and reconfigure its resource base. In fast-moving or hypercompetitive markets (D'Aveni, 1994), the meta-capability to recombine resources may matter more than any single resource — a bridge between RBV and the strategy-as-learning view of Lesson 1.

Porter's value chain

Porter (1985) disaggregates the firm into value-creating activities to locate the sources of cost and differentiation advantage. Primary activities — inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing and sales, and service — directly create and deliver the product. Support activities — firm infrastructure, human-resource management, technology development, and procurement — enable the primary ones. Margin is the difference between total value created and total cost. The analytical payoff often lies in linkages: how the performance of one activity affects the cost or effectiveness of another (e.g., higher-quality inputs reducing inspection and warranty costs), and in horizontal linkages across business units (shared procurement, technology). The value chain also extends outward into the value system linking suppliers and channels — connecting to vertical-integration decisions in Lesson 5.

The IFE matrix

The Internal Factor Evaluation matrix (David & David, 2017) converts the audit into a single comparable score. List 15-20 internal factors (strengths first, then weaknesses); assign weights (0.0-1.0, summing to 1.0) by industry importance; rate each 1-4, where 4 = major strength, 3 = minor strength, 2 = minor weakness, 1 = major weakness; multiply and sum. Totals range 1.0-4.0 around a 2.5 midpoint. A key subtlety: weights reflect importance to the industry while ratings reflect the firm's condition, so a critical weakness still gets a high weight but a low rating. The IFE pairs with the EFE to plot the firm on the IE matrix (Lesson 6).

Worked example

Internal factorWeightRatingScore
Strong brand equity (S)0.2040.80
Proprietary logistics (S)0.1540.60
High employee turnover (W)0.2010.20
Limited R&D budget (W)0.2520.50
Healthy cash position (S)0.2030.60
Total1.002.70

At 2.70 the firm is modestly above average internally, with brand and logistics offsetting an R&D and retention gap that should anchor improvement priorities.

Common mistakes and debates

  • Listing every competence as an "advantage" without VRIO testing.
  • Treating the value chain as an org chart rather than an activity-cost analysis.
  • Ignoring linkages — the richest, least-imitable advantages often live between activities.
  • Confusing IFE ratings (firm condition) with weights (industry importance).
  • Applying static RBV in turbulent markets where dynamic capabilities dominate.

The enduring debate pits Porter's outside-in positioning view (Lesson 2) against Barney's inside-out resource-based view. Most scholars now see them as complementary halves of SWOT: external analysis defines opportunities and threats, internal analysis defines strengths and weaknesses, and strategy formulation (Lesson 6) matches the two. The strengths surfaced here also underpin core competencies (Prahalad & Hamel, 1990) that drive corporate diversification logic in Lesson 5.
